title: Born Into Sharecroppers in Pocahontas
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Jackson, Mississippi, in December 2015 with John Bishop recording, Frankye Adams-Johnson opens by placing her birth in the small town of Pocahontas about fifteen minutes north of Jackson, the daughter of a separated couple, raised in a sharecropping family by her mother, grandparents, and great-grandparents until she was about twelve.

title: From the Country to a Cramped Apartment
summary: Adams-Johnson recounts her mother leaving sharecropping for domestic work in Jackson, eventually buying a home and running a mom-and-pop store, and her own depression at trading the freedom of roaming the woods as a self-described nature girl for a cramped apartment in the city.

title: Out of School for the Crops
summary: She describes attending church schools in Pocahontas through fifth grade, then being bused to a district school in Clinton, and how being kept home to work the crops during planting and harvest seasons became her first realization of what it meant to be Black and poor and a sharecropper.

title: Walking Through the White Strip
summary: She recalls walking through town and past a strip of white houses on the way to her church school, wondering as a child raised in a religious home whether God was white and could possibly like a Black girl, and dates her racial consciousness to 1954 and the murder of Emmett Till.

title: Stories Under the Quilting Frame
summary: She explains how education was stressed in Black families, her blind great-grandmother having taught her to count before she started school, and how, being nosy, she absorbed the realities of race from women's talk under the raised quilting frame and from visiting her mother at the back door of a white household where food was passed out, a scene she likens to the film The Help.

title: The Newest of the Black High Schools
summary: Adams-Johnson describes transferring from junior high to Brinkley High, the newest of Jackson's Black high schools with top-of-the-line equipment and rich activities, and how her life in Jackson shifted with the arrival of the Freedom Rides about a year after the family moved there, around 1959.

title: A Word I Was Clueless About
topic: From Communism Lessons to the Mass Meetings
summary: She recounts a government course warning students against communism to deter involvement, her ignorance of the word leading her to a god brother whose father was active in the NAACP, and the meetings at his house where she met Medgar Evers and, as a starry-eyed high school student around 1962 and 1963, was inspired by three women, Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray.

title: My First Act of Courage
summary: She tells how her first act of courage came in 1963 through college students she was meeting, among them Pearlena Lewis, a North Jackson Youth Council leader who lived in her neighborhood and sat in at Woolworth's, and how her god brother Cleveland and an organizer helped mobilize high school students after the brutal attack on the sit-in.

title: A Quarter Mile From the School
summary: She recounts marching about three days after the Woolworth's attack in response to the beating of Anne Moody and John Salter, students walking out of the three Black high schools including Jim Hill to converge and march downtown, only to be surrounded within a quarter mile by trucks, garbage trucks, and police with guns in a scene she likens to the Twilight Zone.

title: Penned Up Like Livestock
summary: She describes being held on the concrete floor of livestock pens at the fairgrounds, a place she and Medgar Evers likened to a concentration camp, surrounded at night by armed men while the boys formed a protective circle around the girls, and identifies her god brother as Cleveland Donald Jr., a youth leader and the second Black student after James Meredith to attend the University of Mississippi, and her most important connection to the movement.

title: My Summer Job Was Marching
summary: She recalls the summer of 1963 becoming her summer job of marching in protest, reporting daily to the Masonic Temple for her marching orders, and how Medgar Evers's assassination in June deepened her sense of the seriousness and danger of being a freedom fighter, a word that always stirred her lifelong love of freedom.

title: Hit Across the Back in the Paddy Wagon
summary: She describes the emotionally charged march around Medgar Evers's funeral when she was jailed and struck across the back with a billy club after climbing down from a hot paddy wagon to retrieve her dropped writing pad, her fury at being hit by the first man ever to strike her, a white officer, and her struggle to suppress that anger and hold to nonviolent discipline, having once defied even her tyrant grandfather.

title: Medgar Was Like a Father
topic: Jesse Harris, a Void, and the Dogs
summary: She describes the nonviolence training the young people received with Jesse Harris set up as a leader after Medgar Evers's assassination left a void, COFO and SNCC working mostly in the Delta, how Medgar had been like a father always pleading that the young be gotten out of jail, and being in the middle of the funeral march where the conflict with police brought out the dogs and hoses she had seen on the news from Alabama.

title: You Need to Stay Out of That Mess
summary: She recounts slipping out to march while her mother worked, her mother and younger siblings among those swept up at the fairgrounds, and her mother's white employer driving her to retrieve Frankye's brother and threatening to personally beat him if he marched again, an incident that left brother and sister angry that their parents would not stand up.

title: A Music Scholarship to Tougaloo
topic: Arriving in 1964 With the SNCC Workers
summary: She remembers the tear gas she recalls especially on Lynch Street, going to Tougaloo College in 1964 on a music scholarship as a pre-freshman during Freedom Summer, and meeting the full-time SNCC activists there, the challenge that older organizers withheld respect and kept young students off dangerous trips, the bond among six young women including Annie Devine's daughter, and Stokely Carmichael taking them to Greenwood for registration work.

title: Bringing White Friends to the House
topic: Kicked Out and a Mother's Refusal
summary: She tells how her immersion in the movement got her dismissed from the conservatively run pre-freshman program, the Brown University tutors who deepened her involvement, and how, taking her newfound white friends home under her mother's open-door tradition, her mother refused out of fear the whole family could be killed, while Frankye, in her first close intimacy with white people, refused to judge them all by the hatred of the man who hit her.

title: The Quiet One of the Three
summary: She portrays Annie Devine as the quietest and least known of the three fearless women, alongside Victoria Gray and Fannie Lou Hamer, who were always together organizing, and recalls trying to get Devine's daughter Barbara to talk and write about her mother.

title: Not Everyone at Tougaloo Was Progressive
summary: She complicates Tougaloo's reputation as a civil rights hotbed by recalling the conservative pre-freshman director who expelled her for spending too much time with activists, the choir director who made her straighten her hair to perform, a student protest on the lawn of conservative president George A. Owens, and a later chance to process Owens's papers and find his rich collection of files on the movement she had lived as an activist.

title: The Only Trip My Mother Allowed
summary: She describes wanting to look like the SNCC women who traveled to Atlanta and Atlantic City, and the one trip her mother permitted, the March on Washington, riding one of about six buses out of Jackson with ministers and chaperones, her first trip outside Mississippi, where she sat by the monument with her feet in the water amid a sea of people.

title: We Came From Washington, We're Not Taking This
summary: She recounts that on the way home, fired up from Washington, the group integrated the white restroom and lunch counter at the Meridian bus stop, where young white men on motorcycles attacked and a whole-town mob, dressed in black and white though not in Klan uniforms, threw things, until the ministers got between them and the crowd and pleaded with them back onto the bus.

part: Anger, the National Guard, and Leaving Mississippi
title: A Different Kind of Anger
summary: She describes the anger that built after Medgar Evers's assassination and the march on Washington, and the killing of Benjamin Brown around 1967 on Lynch Street, where the National Guard had been brought in, the training that the Guard would not shoot unless fired upon, young and angry marchers throwing things, the Guard opening fire as they ran into an alley, and Brown being killed that evening.

part: New York and a New Consciousness
title: Longing for the Connection
summary: She tells how her alarmed mother sent her to live with her older sister in suburban New York around 1967, where, disconnected from the movement, she began longing for it and attended meetings of a cultural nationalist women's group around Amiri Baraka's wife in White Plains, finding them too intellectual and semantic for her grassroots taste.

title: Black Leather on 125th Street
summary: She describes meeting people in black leather on 125th Street and Seventh Avenue in Harlem around the spring of 1968 whose street-level language and presence drew her in, helping set up a Black Panther Party branch in White Plains with her sister, teaching at a daycare center, and starting a liberation school as she came to believe everyone was becoming politically conscious.

title: They Called It Urban Renewal
summary: She contrasts Mississippi segregation with the deep poverty she found in New York, the urban renewal that moved Black families from their homes into tenements and high-rises, including her sister's home, the conflict over starting a daycare center in their building, and her reassignment by the central committee to the South Bronx as officer of the day.

title: You Can't Put the Panthers in One Bag
summary: Asked about being a woman in the Panthers, she says New York men gave more respect to women's leadership and key positions while California men expected what they called socialistic sex, stresses it was never full equality given the chauvinism, and reflects that the party forgot its own families were part of the people, recounting marrying a Panther, the confrontations with law enforcement, and her four years underground in which she gave birth to her youngest daughter.

title: Free Breakfast and Roaches in Ears
summary: She credits the Panthers with addressing real urban problems beyond civil rights, learning that children went hungry and got roaches in their ears, and names the free breakfast in schools program the Panthers first initiated, rounding up children without breakfast on the premise that hungry children cannot learn.

title: A Guerrilla Woman and Mother Too
summary: She describes the burden on Panther women raising children alone, the dangerous expectation, modeled on the Battle of Algiers, that a woman be a guerrilla and a mother at once, going on the lam with babies, and a male Panther's complaint that she was always bougie because she put her children first.

title: A Decision Against My Better Judgment
summary: She calls going underground a turning point and one of her greatest mistakes, made against her better judgment that she had children, set in the context of the 1971 split in the Black Panther Party that left women especially exposed, coming home to Mississippi to regroup and bringing COINTELPRO danger to her family, returning to the radical wing of the split party, and giving birth to her baby daughter underground in a safe area among white women.

title: Always Looking Over My Shoulder
summary: She describes the life-threatening danger as people around her were killed or jailed, separating herself to survive, networking to build her own support system, getting her two older children back, and after four years exploring her options with a lawyer to clear the situation with a slap on the hand, living always afraid each day might be the day she was caught.

title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly
summary: She tells how her husband's troubles extended her probation to five years, then turns to her message for young people: stand up for something and make a difference as the organized Panthers once gave her purpose, and study the movement without romanticism, learning the good, the bad, and the ugly and finding what works for themselves rather than blindly following, as the interview ends.
